**Pardubice** (Czech pronunciation: [[ˈpardubɪtsɛ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Czech "Help:IPA/Czech") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Cs-Pardubice.ogg "File:Cs-Pardubice.ogg"); [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Pardubitz*) is a city in the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic"). It has about 92,000 inhabitants. It is the capital city of the [Pardubice Region](/wiki/Pardubice_Region "Pardubice Region") and lies on the [Elbe](/wiki/Elbe "Elbe") River. The historic centre is well preserved and is protected as an [urban monument reservation](/wiki/Cultural_monument_(Czech_Republic)#Monument_reservations "Cultural monument (Czech Republic)").

Pardubice is known as a centre of industry, with dominant industries being the [chemical industry](/wiki/Chemical_industry "Chemical industry"), [electrical engineering](/wiki/Electrical_engineering "Electrical engineering") and [mechanical engineering](/wiki/Mechanical_engineering "Mechanical engineering"). The city is also well known for its sporting events, which include the [Velká pardubická](/wiki/Velk%C3%A1_pardubick%C3%A1 "Velká pardubická") (a cross-country [steeplechase](/wiki/Steeplechase_(horse_racing) "Steeplechase (horse racing)")), the [Golden Helmet of Pardubice](/wiki/Golden_Helmet_of_Pardubice "Golden Helmet of Pardubice") in [motorcycle racing](/wiki/Motorcycle_racing "Motorcycle racing"), and the Czech Open international chess and board games festival.

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pardubice&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The name *Pardubice* is derived from the personal Polish name Porydęb. It was the name of the leader of the monks who came here from Poland. For the first time the name was recorded in the form of *Pordobice*.[[4]](#cite_note-jmeno-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pardubice&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Pardubice_n%C3%A1b%C5%99e%C5%BE%C3%AD_Z%C3%A1vodu_m%C3%ADru_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Pardubice_n%C3%A1b%C5%99e%C5%BE%C3%AD_Z%C3%A1vodu_m%C3%ADru_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pardubice_n%C3%A1b%C5%99e%C5%BE%C3%AD_Z%C3%A1vodu_m%C3%ADru_-_panoramio.jpg)

The Elbe River in Pardubice

Pardubice is located about 90 kilometres (56 mi) east of [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague"). The city lies at the confluence of the [Elbe](/wiki/Elbe "Elbe") and [Chrudimka](/wiki/Chrudimka "Chrudimka") rivers. There are several fishponds, artificial lakes and [oxbow lakes](/wiki/Oxbow_lake "Oxbow lake") of the Elbe in the municipal territory.

Pardubice is located in a predominantly flat agricultural lansdscape in the [East Elbe Table](/wiki/East_Elbe_Table "East Elbe Table"), in the eastern part of the [Polabí](/wiki/Polab%C3%AD "Polabí") lowland. The highest point is the hill Stropinský vrch at 258 m (846 ft) above sea level, located on the southeastern municipal border.

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pardubice&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Climate")]

Pardubice's climate is classified as [oceanic climate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *Cfb*; [Trewartha](/wiki/Trewartha_climate_classification "Trewartha climate classification"): *Dobo*). Among them, the annual average temperature is 9.7 °C (49.5 °F), the hottest month in July is 19.8 °C (67.6 °F), and the coldest month is −0.3 °C (31.5 °F) in January. The annual precipitation is 573.4 millimetres (22.57 in), of which July is the wettest with 79.9 millimetres (3.15 in), while February is the driest with only 30.9 millimetres (1.22 in). The extreme temperature throughout the year ranged from −27.7 °C (−17.9 °F) on 29 December 1996 to 37.7 °C (99.9 °F) on 28 July 2013.[[6]](#cite_note-extremes-6)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pardubice&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Karte_von_Pardubitz.tif/lossy-page1-250px-Karte_von_Pardubitz.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Karte_von_Pardubitz.tif)

Map of Pardubice (c. 1760)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Pardubice%2C_n%C3%A1m%C4%9Bst%C3%AD_Republiky%2C_kostel_svat%C3%A9ho_Bartolom%C4%9Bje.jpg/250px-Pardubice%2C_n%C3%A1m%C4%9Bst%C3%AD_Republiky%2C_kostel_svat%C3%A9ho_Bartolom%C4%9Bje.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pardubice,_n%C3%A1m%C4%9Bst%C3%AD_Republiky,_kostel_svat%C3%A9ho_Bartolom%C4%9Bje.jpg)

Church of Saint Bartholomew on Republic Square

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Zamek_pce_2.jpg/250px-Zamek_pce_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zamek_pce_2.jpg)

Pardubice Castle

The first written mention of Pardubice is from 1295, when [Pope Boniface VIII](/wiki/Pope_Boniface_VIII "Pope Boniface VIII") took over the protection of the local Church of Saint Bartholomew with the order of [Canons Regular of the Penitence of the Blessed Martyrs](/wiki/Canons_Regular_of_the_Penitence_of_the_Blessed_Martyrs "Canons Regular of the Penitence of the Blessed Martyrs"). In the first half of the 14th century, the settlement was acquired by a noble family, later known as Lords of Pardubice. In 1340, when Pardubice was inherited by [Arnošt of Pardubice](/wiki/Arno%C5%A1t_of_Pardubice "Arnošt of Pardubice"), it was first referred to as a city.[[4]](#cite_note-jmeno-4)[[10]](#cite_note-history-10)

In 1491, Pardubice was bought by [Vilém II of Pernštejn](/wiki/Vil%C3%A9m_II_of_Pern%C5%A1tejn "Vilém II of Pernštejn"), who continued to expand the city and made significant impact on its prosperity. He chose Pardubice as the centre of his estate and began to build a city that corresponded to his status. The [Pernštejn family](/wiki/Pern%C5%A1tejn_family "Pernštejn family") had the entire historic centre with the Renaissance square built. They also had rebuilt the medieval castle into a comfortable fortified residence. In 1560, the Pernštejns were forced to sell the estate to Emperor [Ferdinand I](/wiki/Ferdinand_I,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Ferdinand I, Holy Roman Emperor") because of debts.[[10]](#cite_note-history-10)[[11]](#cite_note-hist2-11)

During the [Thirty Years' War](/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_War "Thirty Years' War"), the city was besieged by the army of General [Lennart Torstensson](/wiki/Lennart_Torstensson "Lennart Torstensson"), but was not conquered.[[10]](#cite_note-history-10)

An important milestone in the history of the city was the year 1845, when the railway from [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague") to [Olomouc](/wiki/Olomouc "Olomouc") was finished. Pardubice became an important railway junction, which led to the development of the food, engineering and chemical industries, and subsequently the development of social and cultural life. In 1874, the [Velká pardubická](/wiki/Velk%C3%A1_pardubick%C3%A1 "Velká pardubická") steeplechase horse race took place for the first time. In 1910, [Jan Kašpar](/wiki/Jan_Ka%C5%A1par "Jan Kašpar") made here the first successful flight in Czech lands and a year later he made history by flying the first [long-haul flight](/wiki/Flight_length "Flight length") from Pardubice to Prague.[[10]](#cite_note-history-10)[[11]](#cite_note-hist2-11)

Until 1918, the city was part of [Austria Hungary](/wiki/Austria_Hungary "Austria Hungary"). The seat of the Pardubice district, it was one of the 94 *[Bezirkshauptmannschaften](/wiki/Bezirkshauptmannschaften "Bezirkshauptmannschaften")* in [Bohemia](/wiki/Bohemia "Bohemia").

In the interwar period, Pardubice continued to develop until its heyday ended with the advent of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). During the war, the city was damaged by air strikes of the Allies, and the Fanto Werke refinery was repeatedly bombed during the [oil campaign](/wiki/Oil_campaign_chronology_of_World_War_II "Oil campaign chronology of World War II").[[10]](#cite_note-history-10)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pardubice&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Foxconn_Pardubice.JPG/250px-Foxconn_Pardubice.JPG)](/wiki/File:Foxconn_Pardubice.JPG)

Foxconn Czech Republic

Pardubice is known as an industrial city. The dominant industries are chemical industry, electrical engineering and mechanical engineering. [Tesla](/wiki/Tesla_(Czechoslovak_company) "Tesla (Czechoslovak company)") electronics manufacturer operated here in 1921–1989. In 2000, [Foxconn](/wiki/Foxconn "Foxconn") established a production plant in its former premises. This electronics manufacturer is the largest employer in Pardubice.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Pardubice has a long tradition in the chemical industry. It is represented by the Paramo refinery, which was founded by David Fanto in 1889. Today it is owned by [Orlen Unipetrol](/wiki/Orlen_Unipetrol "Orlen Unipetrol").[[15]](#cite_note-15) In Pardubice-Semtín there are the companies [Explosia](/wiki/Explosia_a.s. "Explosia a.s.") (a manufacturer of explosives founded in 1920, which is associated with the invention of [Semtex](/wiki/Semtex "Semtex") plastic explosive) and its subsidiary Synthesia (manufacturer of cellulose, pigments and dyes, and organic compounds, established in 1929).[[16]](#cite_note-16)

For centuries, Pardubice have been known for the production of [gingerbread](/wiki/Gingerbread "Gingerbread"). The gingerbread guild was established in the 16th century.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The first factory was founded in 1913. Since 2008, Pardubice gingerbread is a [protected geographical indication by the European Union](/wiki/Geographical_indications_and_traditional_specialities_in_the_European_Union "Geographical indications and traditional specialities in the European Union").[[18]](#cite_note-18)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pardubice&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Transport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Pardubice_hlavn%C3%AD_n%C3%A1dra%C5%BE%C3%AD_%28by_Pudelek%29.jpg/250px-Pardubice_hlavn%C3%AD_n%C3%A1dra%C5%BE%C3%AD_%28by_Pudelek%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pardubice_hlavn%C3%AD_n%C3%A1dra%C5%BE%C3%AD_(by_Pudelek).jpg)

[Pardubice hlavní nádraží](/wiki/Pardubice_hlavn%C3%AD_n%C3%A1dra%C5%BE%C3%AD "Pardubice hlavní nádraží") railway station

Pardubice is located at the crossroads of three main roads: the I/2, which connects Pardubice with [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague"); the I/36 from Pardubice to [Rychnov nad Kněžnou District](/wiki/Rychnov_nad_Kn%C4%9B%C5%BEnou_District "Rychnov nad Kněžnou District"); and the I/37, which connects [Hradec Králové](/wiki/Hradec_Kr%C3%A1lov%C3%A9 "Hradec Králové") with [Žďár nad Sázavou](/wiki/%C5%BD%C4%8F%C3%A1r_nad_S%C3%A1zavou "Žďár nad Sázavou") and the [D1 motorway](/wiki/D1_motorway_(Czech_Republic) "D1 motorway (Czech Republic)").

The railway station [Pardubice hlavní nádraží](/wiki/Pardubice_hlavn%C3%AD_n%C3%A1dra%C5%BE%C3%AD "Pardubice hlavní nádraží") is an important railway junction. Pardubice has direct railway connection with many cities, including Prague, [Brno](/wiki/Brno "Brno"), [Olomouc](/wiki/Olomouc "Olomouc"), [Ostrava](/wiki/Ostrava "Ostrava") and [Liberec](/wiki/Liberec "Liberec") in the Czech Republic, [Bratislava](/wiki/Bratislava "Bratislava"), [Žilina](/wiki/%C5%BDilina "Žilina") and [Košice](/wiki/Ko%C5%A1ice "Košice") in Slovakia, [Wrocław](/wiki/Wroc%C5%82aw "Wrocław"), [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków"), [Poznań](/wiki/Pozna%C5%84 "Poznań"), [Gdańsk](/wiki/Gda%C5%84sk "Gdańsk"), [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw") and [Gdynia](/wiki/Gdynia "Gdynia") in Poland, [Budapest](/wiki/Budapest "Budapest") in Hungary, and [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna") and [Graz](/wiki/Graz "Graz") in Austria. The territory of Pardubice is served by eight train stations and stops.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Pardubice is served by [Pardubice Airport](/wiki/Pardubice_Airport "Pardubice Airport"), which is used as both military and civilian international airport.

In addition to buses, [trolleybuses](/wiki/Trolleybus "Trolleybus") also provide intra-city transport. Trolleybus service was started in 1952.[[20]](#cite_note-20) There are 12 trolleybus lines in operation.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Intra-city transport is provided by the company Dopravní podnik města Pardubic a.s., which is owned by the city of Pardubice. The company was founded in 1950.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pardubice&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Education")]

Pardubice is home to [University of Pardubice](/wiki/University_of_Pardubice "University of Pardubice"). It was founded in 1950 as Chemical College and is mainly focused on fields historically associated with the city, such as the chemical industry and transportation.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pardubice&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Sport")]

The city was first represented in the top national football competition by [SK Pardubice](/wiki/SK_Pardubice "SK Pardubice") in the 1930s and 1940s. From 1957 to 1960, [Dukla Pardubice](/wiki/Dukla_Pardubice "Dukla Pardubice") played in the top tier. Later [VCHZ Pardubice](/wiki/VCHZ_Pardubice "VCHZ Pardubice") played in the top national league in the 1968–69 season. Nowadays the city is represented by [FK Pardubice](/wiki/FK_Pardubice "FK Pardubice"), which plays in the [Czech First League](/wiki/Czech_First_League "Czech First League"). Women's team plays in the [Czech Women's First League](/wiki/Czech_Women%27s_First_League "Czech Women's First League").

The ice hockey club [HC Dynamo Pardubice](/wiki/HC_Dynamo_Pardubice "HC Dynamo Pardubice") plays in the [Czech Extraliga](/wiki/Czech_Extraliga "Czech Extraliga"). The team plays its home games at [Enteria arena](/wiki/Enteria_arena_(Pardubice) "Enteria arena (Pardubice)").

The basketball team is [BK JIP Pardubice](/wiki/BK_Pardubice "BK Pardubice"), playing in the [National Basketball League](/wiki/National_Basketball_League_(Czech_Republic) "National Basketball League (Czech Republic)").

The Pardubice Stallions are an American football team currently playing in the [Czech League of American Football](/wiki/Czech_League_of_American_Football "Czech League of American Football") 2nd division. They play home games at the Summer Stadium.

Pardubice is also represented in the Czech [rink bandy](/wiki/Rink_bandy "Rink bandy") league.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Pardubice hosts two world-famous sporting events each year. The [Velká pardubická](/wiki/Velk%C3%A1_pardubick%C3%A1 "Velká pardubická") steeplechase was first held in 1874 and is one of the most famous horse races in Europe. The second event is the [Golden Helmet of Pardubice](/wiki/Golden_Helmet_of_Pardubice "Golden Helmet of Pardubice"), a [motorcycle speedway](/wiki/Motorcycle_speedway "Motorcycle speedway") competition held at the [Svítkov Stadium](/wiki/Sv%C3%ADtkov_Stadium "Svítkov Stadium"). The Golden Helmet has been run since 1929 is one of the oldest individual titles in world speedway.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) [AMK Zlatá Přilba Pardubice](/wiki/AMK_Zlat%C3%A1_P%C5%99ilba_Pardubice "AMK Zlatá Přilba Pardubice") is a speedway team that race at the Svítkov Stadium.

The Czech Open international chess and games festival has been held in Pardubice since 1990.[[27]](#cite_note-27) The city was also known for the [Czech Open](/wiki/Czech_Open_(golf) "Czech Open (golf)") in golf, but it was last held in 2011.

## Sights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pardubice&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Sights")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Pardubice%2C_t%C5%99%C3%ADda_M%C3%ADru%2C_k_Zelen%C3%A9_br%C3%A1n%C4%9B.jpg/250px-Pardubice%2C_t%C5%99%C3%ADda_M%C3%ADru%2C_k_Zelen%C3%A9_br%C3%A1n%C4%9B.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pardubice,_t%C5%99%C3%ADda_M%C3%ADru,_k_Zelen%C3%A9_br%C3%A1n%C4%9B.jpg)

View towards the Green Gate

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Pardubice%2C_Komensk%C3%A9ho_n%C3%A1m%C4%9Bst%C3%AD%2C_kostel_Zv%C4%9Bstov%C3%A1n%C3%AD_Panny_Marie.jpg/250px-Pardubice%2C_Komensk%C3%A9ho_n%C3%A1m%C4%9Bst%C3%AD%2C_kostel_Zv%C4%9Bstov%C3%A1n%C3%AD_Panny_Marie.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pardubice,_Komensk%C3%A9ho_n%C3%A1m%C4%9Bst%C3%AD,_kostel_Zv%C4%9Bstov%C3%A1n%C3%AD_Panny_Marie.jpg)

Church of the Annunciation of the Virgin Mary on the Komenského Square

The landmark and symbol of Pardubice is the Green Gate as a remnant of the city's fortifications. It is a Renaissance gate with a façade decorated by a relief designed by [Mikoláš Aleš](/wiki/Mikol%C3%A1%C5%A1_Ale%C5%A1 "Mikoláš Aleš"), which depicts the Lords of Pardubice. Behind the gate is a 60-metre-high (200 ft) tower that serves as a lookout tower. In its interior there is also an exposition with the history and legends of the city.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

Pardubice Castle was built at the end of the 13th century and rebuilt in the Renaissance style at the turn of the 15th and 16th centuries. Massive fortifications are preserved around the castle. Today the castle houses the Museum of East Bohemia in Pardubice and Gallery of East Bohemia in Pardubice.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

The original Church of Saint Bartholomew was built in 1295 and destroyed during the [Hussite Wars](/wiki/Hussite_Wars "Hussite Wars"). The today's Church of Saint Bartholomew was built together with a monastery in 1507–1514. It was used as a burial place of the [Pernštejn family](/wiki/Pern%C5%A1tejn_family "Pernštejn family"). In the interior there is a valuable main altar with the painting "The Passion of St. Bartholomew" from 1692 by [Michael Willmann](/wiki/Michael_Willmann "Michael Willmann") and a painting decoration by Mikoláš Aleš.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

The Church of the Annunciation of the Virgin Mary was founded by [Arnošt of Pardubice](/wiki/Arno%C5%A1t_of_Pardubice "Arnošt of Pardubice") before 1359. Its present late Gothic and Renaissance appearance dates from the first half of the 16th century. Until 1786, the church belonged to a Minorite monastery, from which time there are extensive underground tombs under the church.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

The House at Jonáš is one of the most treasured burgher houses in Pardubice. It was built after the fire that hit the city in 1507. It is known for its façade, decorated with a stucco relief from 1797, showing a biblical scene of a whale swallowing the prophet [Jonah](/wiki/Jonah "Jonah"). The premises of the house are used by the Gallery of East Bohemia in Pardubice.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

The Zámeček Memorial is a place of reverence that commemorates execution of 194 people in 1942. The place is a national cultural monument and at its centrepiece is a granite monument dating from 1949.[[33]](#cite_note-33)
